answer:
**Charles Dunoyer's *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, Chapter 16 ("VI. De la liberté de l'industrie manufacturière" / "VI. On the liberty of the manufacturing industry").**[^1]

Dunoyer argues against the view that industrial progress derives primarily from scientific theory, emphasizing instead empirical methods by "groping" inventors. He stacks three biographical examples in succession:

> Arkwright, the author of the spinning machine, one of the most ingenious and powerful to have been invented in fifty years, **the barber Arkwright had no scientific instruction, and judged only by groping, by trying, by experimenting,** on the effect that each of the pieces of his admirable machine would produce.[^2]

> **Newcomen and Cawley, two mechanics who contributed appreciably to the progress of the steam engine, and the first who sought and succeeded in drawing useful effects from it, were, one a hardware dealer, the other a blacksmith,** and neither could pass for a scholar.[^3]

> **Watt himself, the illustrious Watt, when he began to make that series of perfections to the steam engine which have made it so powerful a motor, must not have possessed much scientific knowledge**... **when, in 1764, [he] was charged by the University of Glasgow... with repairing a Newcomen machine, it was simply in the capacity of a craftsman,** [having] been able to study only until the age of sixteen in one of those elementary and free schools that exist in Scotland under the name of *Grammar school.*[^4]

These examples illustrate his broader claim: "the two most admirable instruments, perhaps, that human industry possesses, the spinning machine and the fire-engine, could be, the first invented by a man absolutely deprived of scientific instruction, and the second much perfected by men little or not at all versed in theory."[^5]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [Chapter 16](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [p-b376b2](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-b376b2).

[^3]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [p-56a704](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-56a704).

[^4]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [p-e35bab](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-e35bab).

[^5]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [p-1974eb](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-1974eb).
